justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('redefines what a Shipment IS'), minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1), data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-6, business_impact.risk), external API breakage identified (BR-8, AC-4), delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced (escalation 4), architectural concern flagged via escalations, and confidence.level=62 which is <=75.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — identified the cardinality change as a fundamental breaking change to the core aggregate, not just a schema tweak
BR-1 explicitly states the minimum-one-Recipient invariant with strong justification
BR-8 directly addresses external partner API backward compatibility as an invariant constraint
Data migration of 2M records called out in BR-6, business_impact, and confidence reasoning
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as a dedicated escalation with concrete options and recommendation
Confidence level of 62 appropriately reflects the many undefined business decisions
Goes well beyond minimum requirements — identifies billing model ambiguity, SLA granularity, cancellation granularity, max recipient limit, and partial delivery states

Edge cases are exceptionally well-considered: same-address different preferences, mid-route changes, in-flight modifications, billing model, reporting impact, cancellation granularity, SLA calculation
Ubiquitous language includes emergent concepts like 'Delivery Stop' and notes Recipient's transition from value object to entity — shows deep DDD understanding
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive across 8 groups with specific impacts per group
